blocks.content: Nathan and Maya find them minutes later.

Maya reads the scene before she reaches it. She has been reading Cassandra her entire life — the particular grammar of her posture, the emotional weather in her face. She sees it from twenty feet away: the redness, the openness, the quality of having been somewhere very deep and come back from it.
She doesn't say anything. She goes straight to her sister and puts her hand on her shoulder — steady, warm, claiming — and leaves it there.
Cassandra reaches up and covers Maya's hand with hers.
That's all. No words. The sisters speaking in the language they've always had, the one that doesn't require sound.

Nathan holds the penguin. He reads the room with his particular precision — he is funnier than people give him credit for and smarter than his timing suggests, and what he reads now is: something real happened here. Something that deserves space.
Then, because he is Nathan, he gives it approximately thirty seconds before he opens his arms wide.
